intentionally minimal — prompt text and a timestamp, no result
payload, no error record. Failed runs are recorded the same way
as successful ones because the UI typically wants to re-run or
tweak a prompt regardless of how it ended.

### Scope — what lives here and what does not

Per architecture §12 ("State Ownership"), these three slices are
**Core-owned** Studio-level concerns that are neither Puck's
(

, drag/drop) nor the host's (credentials, feature
flags). Plugin-local state stays inside each plugin factory; the
host's

), not through a store.

### Plugin write path

**Plugins never import these hooks.** The write path is lifecycle
events — plugins emit

) subscribes
to those events and calls the appropriate store setter. Keeping the
write path indirect is what lets plugins stay decoupled from the
Studio shell — a plugin that imported

directly
would break in any host that composed it without a

root.

Uses the v5 curried form —

Full shape of the export store. Split into read fields (the state
snapshot) and setter actions (the write surface).

Actions live alongside state on the same object rather than under
a nested

sub-key because the surface is small and
consumers read it via selectors. If the store grows past a dozen
fields, consider splitting into context / actions like the
Zustand v5 "split state and actions" pattern.

runtime, in plugin insertion order. Populated from
); tests pass a synthetic list directly.

if nothing is selected yet. Persisted across reloads so the

field

while an export run is in flight. **Never persisted** —
a reload always lands on

so the UI can never be stuck
in a spinner that outlives the request it belonged to.

before any run has
completed. Ephemeral — a reload clears it.

lifecycle handler (successful

summary.text:
owns the config factory call so descendants get a
single

AI block. Intentionally carries **no credentials** —
API keys, endpoint URLs, and auth tokens belong to the
host backend, and the AI copilot plugin factory receives
a

callback that wraps whatever network
path the host has set up. Architecture §9 is explicit
about this boundary.

Optional hint for the AI plugin about which model
to request by default (e.g.

Maximum number of retries for a failed AI
generation call. Clamped to

so hosts
cannot accidentally enable unbounded retry
storms. Defaults to

Branding block for the editor chrome — app name, logo,
and accent color surfaced in the Studio header.

Display name shown in the editor header and the
browser tab. Defaults to

so an
un-configured host still looks intentional.

Optional URL of a logo rendered in the header.
Validated as a URL via

(top-level format
in Zod v4). Omit to fall back to the default
wordmark.

Optional primary accent color for the editor
chrome. Free-form string so hosts can pass a
CSS variable reference (

Brand-kit block (I3-4) — shared colors + font families the
Canvas Studio editor surfaces (color pickers, font menus) so
designs stay on-brand. Distinct from

is design content the canvas
reads via its
maps these entries to the editor's

. The
first entry is treated as the brand default. Empty by
default.

Export pipeline block — configuration for the export
registry and download flow.

Optional id of the format to pre-select in export
menus (e.g.

). Resolved at
runtime against the registered format registry —
an id that does not match any registered format
falls back to the first registered format.

Filename prefix for downloads. The export pipeline
appends the format's extension (e.g.

— hosts
typically override with a slug derived from the
current page title.
Feature flag block. Every flag defaults to

so a
fresh install has no surprise behavior enabled — hosts opt
in explicitly.

Placeholder for post-alpha work — no collaboration

Theme block — the initial color mode and whether the
built-in theme toggle is shown.

, the built-in theme toggle button is
rendered in the header. Hosts that manage theme
externally can set this to

and drive the
theme via their own controls.

respects the user's
OS preference via

force the

Initial Puck page data. Forwarded to

also captured into a ref so
Drives the "save draft" button's loading state.
Timestamp of the last successful save (relative-time hint).

Optional diagnostics sink for plugin log records and Studio setup
failures. Metadata is shallow-redacted before delivery.
Optional "back" handler for the chrome header. Hidden when absent.

Optional handler invoked from the publish panel's Export submenu
with the format id. The host normalizes Puck data to

, and
triggers the download. The submenu disables itself when omitted.

Optional click handler for the panel's "Publish to live" action.
Disabled when omitted. The Puck

pipeline still fires
through Puck's own publish UI; this exists so the AnvilKit chrome
can offer a header button wired to the host's publish flow.
Optional "save draft" handler; header surfaces a button when set.

Consumer-supplied Puck overrides. Composed **outermost** —
after the plugins' overrides — so the consumer always has the
final word on any given slot.

plugins) to mount.
Order matters for override composition: the first plugin's
overrides become the innermost wrapper, later plugins wrap it.

is required by the spec, but we add one for

and updated on every

Restore every field to its initial state. Present for

Update the user's preference. Typically called from the
header's theme-toggle button.

Update the resolved on-screen value. Called once at mount
with

creates the instance
itself (so it can call

imperatively) and passes it
in; standalone callers omit it and the provider owns one.

Diagnostic projection of the compiled runtime, exposed to host
apps and plugin UIs.

Kept deliberately small: the fields here are all read-only

).
Any mutation path goes through the Zustand stores or through
's own lifecycle wiring — a consumer calling

cannot trigger a publish, a data change, or an

host app the same list

exposes, minus the reactivity — consumers that need reactive

that successfully
registered, in declaration order. Suitable for a "plugins

Compose a list of plugin-contributed Puck override slices into a
single

dictionary, with per-key currying so
multiple plugins touching the same key all run.

The input array is consumed in order: earlier entries become
**innermost** wrappers (closer to the default render), later
entries become **outermost**. This matches Puck 0.21's own
internal composition so feeding the result back into

is semantically identical to letting Puck
register each plugin individually — just done once, upfront, so

can also fold in consumer-supplied overrides in the
same pass.
